coredata.dc:title: Solvent-free synthesis of recyclable hydrophobic carbon dots for highly selective detecting curcumin in food samples
coredata.dc:creator: He, Mengyao
Jiang, Xiuming
Wang, Xinyu
Xiang, Guoqiang
He, Lijun
coredata.dc:description: A highly selective fluorometric method was developed for the specific determination of curcumin (Cur) based on hydrophobic carbon dots (HB-CDs) derived from flaxseed oil by a solvent-free reaction. The as-prepared HB-CDs possessed blue fluorescence emission (at 425 nm) with a fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of 21.1 %. It was that the fluorescence of HB-CDs could be quantitatively quenched by Cur, and the fluorescence quenching mechanism was proved to be a combination of dynamic and static quenching. The detection limit of the method for Cur was 20 nM with a linear response range of 0.2–200 µM. Additionally, HB-CDs can be quickly recovered from the tested solutions through solvent change precipitation. The developed HB-CDs based fluorescent method has been successfully applied for real food samples analysis with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Flaxseed oil
Hydrophobic carbon dots
Curcumin
Recyclable
Food samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of Cur in food samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Innovative Funds Plan of Henan University of Technology (Grant No. 2020ZKCJ03 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (a) and high-resolution TEM (b) images of HB-CDs; FT-IR spectrum (c) of HB-CDs; UV–Vis absorption spectrum (d) of HB-CDs; High resolution XPS spectra for C1s (e) and O1s (f).

(a) Emission spectra of HB-CDs with excitation wavelengths varied from 310 to 390 nm; (b) Effect of NaOH concentration on the fluorescence intensity of HB-CDs; (c) Effect of oleic acid concentration on the fluorescence intensity of HB-CDs.
(a) Effects of HB-CDs concentrations on the fluorescence quenching efficiency ((I0-I)/I0) and (b) Effects of incubation time on the fluorescence intensity of HB-CDs and HB-CDs-Cur.

The quantitatively fluorescence quenching behavior by Cur. (a) Fluorescence response of HB-CDs for different concentration of Cur; (b) Linear relationship between lg(I0/I) and Cur concentrations (0.2–200 µM).
Effect of ethanol-soluble interferents on Cur detection (Concentrations for the interferents are 0.1 mM, the added Cur concentration is 5 μM).

(a) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of HB-CDs、and UV–Vis absorption spectrum of Cur. (b) Fluorescence decay curves of HB-CDs in the absence and presence of Cur (5 μM). (c) Fluorescence decay curves of HB-CDs in the absence and presence of Cur (50 μM). (d) Stern-Volmer plot of I0/I versus Cur concentrations.
Schematic illustration of synthesis of HB-CDs by solvent-free thermal treatment and the detection of Cur with recyclable HB-CDs.
Analytical performance for the reported CDs-based methods for Cur.
Analytical results for Cur in different food samples (n = 3, mg g−1).

head.ce:title: Solvent-free synthesis of recyclable hydrophobic carbon dots for highly selective detecting curcumin in food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan University of Technology, Zhengzhou 450001, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan University of Technology, Zhengzhou 450001, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Key Laboratory of Cereal and Oil Food Safety Inspection and Control, Zhengzhou, Henan 450001, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Key Laboratory of Cereal and Oil Food Safety Inspection and Control

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Henan Key Laboratory of Cereal and Oil Food Safety Inspection and Control, Zhengzhou, Henan 450001, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A highly selective fluorometric method was developed for the specific determination of curcumin (Cur) based on hydrophobic carbon dots (HB-CDs) derived from flaxseed oil by a solvent-free reaction. The as-prepared HB-CDs possessed blue fluorescence emission (at 425 nm) with a fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of 21.1 %. It was that the fluorescence of HB-CDs could be quantitatively quenched by Cur, and the fluorescence quenching mechanism was proved to be a combination of dynamic and static quenching. The detection limit of the method for Cur was 20 nM with a linear response range of 0.2–200 µM. Additionally, HB-CDs can be quickly recovered from the tested solutions through solvent change precipitation. The developed HB-CDs based fluorescent method has been successfully applied for real food samples analysis with satisfactory results.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Curcumin (Cur) is a kind of natural polyphenol in rhizomes of turmeric [1–3] with a content ranging from 0.5 % to 5.7 % [4]. Now, the World Health Organization (WHO) has identified Cur as the natural additive for food, cosmetics, dietary and herbal supplements due to its unique therapeutic properties, such as antioxidant [5], antibacterial [6], anti-inflammatory [7], and anti-carcinogenic [8]. However, some studies have found that excessive intake of Cur has a pro-oxidation effect on DNA, which eventually lead to cell necrosis and damage human health [9]. WHO recommended that the daily dosage of Cur as a food additive should not exceed 0.3 mg kg−1 [10]. Therefore, it was important to establish a rapid, sensitive and cost-efficient analytical method for Cur in food samples.

A wide variety of analytical methods have been developed for the quantification of Cur in different matrices, such as spectrophotometry [11,12], fluorimetry [13,14], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [15], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [16] and electrochemical techniques [17,18]. Chromatography requires expensive instruments, extended analysis time and high cost; electrodes used in electrochemical methods often require complex and cumbersome processes. In contrast, the fluorescence method has attracted much attention because of its simple operation, high sensitivity and fast response[19,20]. As a new type of fluorescent nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have been successfully applied to the fluorescence sensing Cur in food and biological samples [21–25].

These reported CDs for sensing Cur are water-soluble, and all the sensing procedures are performed in water [22] or water/ethanol media [9,25]. However, Cur is an oil-soluble substance, which indicated that the most popular extractant for Cur from solid samples is organic solvent (such as ethanol, methanol) [26–28]. Although aqueous alkali is also an option [29], the extraction efficiency of aqueous alkali extraction is much lower than that of alcohol extraction [30]. Therefore, the fluorescence method for Cur based on water-soluble CDs will face the problem of inconsistency between the detection media and the Cur extractant. The use of water/ethanol mixed solvents is a popular compromise solution [9,25,27,28], but it may still face the problem of CDs solvent effect. Obviously, the application of hydrophobic fluorescent probes for water-insoluble analytes is a perfect solution. Recently, researchers have made some attempts at the synthesis and analytical applications of hydrophobic CDs (HB-CDs) [31–33]. Solvent-free thermal treatment is a practical, green synthesis strategy for HB-CDs [32].

Herein, HB-CDs were prepared by a solvent-free thermal treatment using flaxseed oil as a single carbon source. The emission spectrum of HB-CDs (peak at 425 nm) overlaps seriously with the absorption band of Cur (peak at 430 nm), resulting in an obvious fluorescence quenching of HB-CDs by Cur. Based on the quenching phenomenon, a sensitive and selective fluorescence sensing method in ethanol medium for Cur was developed. HB-CDs could be easily recycled from the tested solutions and reused. The fluorescence quenching mechanism was confirmed to be a combination of dynamic and static quenching. The developed fluorescence method was successfully applied to the determination of Cur in food samples with satisfactory spiking recoveries.

The optical properties of HB-CDs were investigated by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu, Japan) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-6000, Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescence lifetime of HB-CDs was measured on an FLS980 Series of Fluorescence Spectrometers (Edinburgh Instruments, France). The morphology, elemental composition and surface groups were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100plus, Japan), X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, K-Alpha, Thermo Scientific, USA) and FT-IR spectrometer (WQF-530, Beijing Beifen-Ruili Analytical Instrument, China), respectively.

Cur, fructose (FRU), chloramphenicol (CPL), roxithromycin (ROX), malic acid (MA), citric acid (CA), tocopherol (VE), absolute ethanol and sulfates or nitrites of metals were purchased from Xiya Reagent (Shandong, China) with analytical pure level.

HB-CDs were successfully fabricated using flaxseed oil as the sole precursor through a solvent-free hydrothermal procedure. Detail: 12 mL of flaxseed oil was transferred to a Teflon autoclave (100 mL), sealed and heated at 240 °C for 12 h. After the reaction was completed, the obtained yellow–brown liquid (about 10 mL) was mixed with absolute ethanol (40 mL). After sufficient oscillation extraction, ethanol-soluble HB-CDs entered the ethanol phase. The obtained HB-CDs ethanol solution was collected, and the concentration of HB-CDs was calculated to be about 100 mg mL−1 by the mass difference of the reaction product (yellowish-brown liquid) before and after ethanol extraction.

Seasoning powder and Cur related foods (including curry powder, ginger powder and mustard powder, turmeric sugar, turmeric milk powder, ginger powder and turmeric powder, turmeric powder solid beverage) were purchased from local supermarkets (Zhengzhou, China). Cur was extracted from actual food samples using absolute ethanol as extractant according to the procedure recommended by national standard of China (GB 1886.76–2015). Briefly, 0.1 g of food sample was homogenized and mixed with 5 mL absolute ethanol in a centrifuge tube. After 20 min of ultrasonic treatment, the supernatant was separated by centrifugation (10000 rpm, 10 min) and stored for testing.

First, 1 mL of HB-CDs ethanol solution (100 mg mL−1) was placed in a graduated tube (5.0 mL). Then a certain amount of Cur standard solution (or sample solution) was added to the tube and diluted with absolute ethanol to the desired volume. At the same time, the blank solution was prepared similarly without adding Cur standard solution. The fluorescence intensity (425 nm) of the solution (I) and the blank solution (I0) was directly recorded under the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The calculated lg (I0/I) was used for spectrometric quantification.

Fig. 1 a illustrates the TEM images of HB-CDs with particle sizes of 1.5–5.5 nm. The HR-TEM image (Fig. 1b) showed the formation of crystalline HB-CDs with paralleled and ordered lattice fringes. The lattice d-spacing of the as-synthesized HB-CDs is found to be 0.20 nm, which corresponds to (1102) faces [34].

FT-IR spectrum was used to confirm the presence of functional groups on the surface of HB-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1c, the firm absorption peaks at 3029 cm−1 and 1816 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of O-H and -C = O(–COOH), respectively. The absorption peaks at 1218 cm−1, 1022 cm−1 and 771 cm−1 are assigned to the C-C stretching vibration, C-O stretching vibration and C-H bending vibration, respectively. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of HB-CDs were showed in Fig. 1d, and it exhibits a prominent shoulder peak at about 280 nm, which is ascribable to the π-π* transition from C = C bond [24].

XPS was used to confirm the elemental composition of HB-CDs. It can be known from the XPS full scan results that HB-CDs are mainly composed of C and O elements, and the atomic proportions are 88.1 % and 11.43 %, respectively (Fig. S1). In the high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 1e), the four peaks at 285.0, 285.4 and 288.7 eV can be assigned to C-C/C = C, C-O, C = O [35–37], respectively. In the high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 1f), the three peaks at 531.8, 532.9, 536.3 eV can be assigned to O = C, O-C, C-OH [35,38], respectively. The elemental information obtained by XPS is consistent with that obtained by FT-IR.

The fluorescence emission behavior of HB-CDs showed evident excitation wavelength dependence. (Fig. 2 a). With the increase of excitation wavelength (310–390 nm), the fluorescence emission wavelength showed a significant redshift, while the fluorescence intensity decreased after increasing to the maximum. Under the excitation wavelength of 350 nm, HB-CDs possess the maximum fluorescence emission peak at 425 nm with a fluorescence quantum yield of 21.1 % (quinine sulfate as the reference).

The fluorescence of HB-CDs in NaOH or oleic acid medium was recorded to investigate the acid-base response properties of HB-CDs. In NaOH medium, the fluorescence of HB-CDs decreased rapidly with NaOH concentration. When the NaOH concentration was more significant than 0.2 M, the fluorescence intensity reached the lowest stable value (Fig. 2b). This may be explained by the neutralization reaction of the –COOH group on the surface of HB-CDs with NaOH. In oleic acid medium, the fluorescence intensity of HB-CDs showed a linear decrease with the increase of oleic acid concentration (Fig. 2c). This may be related to the change of solvent polarity caused by high concentration of oleic acid.

It was found that Cur could significantly quench the fluorescence of HB-CDs when Cur was added to the HB-CDs ethanol solution. Thus, HB-CDs can serve as a fluorescence probe for monitoring Cur. And the HB-CDs based fluorescence method was well optimized. The effects of HB-CDs concentration and incubation time on the fluorescence response of HB-CDs to Cur were investigated. As shown in Fig. 3 a, it is clear that the fluorescence quenching efficiency ((I0-I)/I0) gradually of HB-CDs increases with the increase of HB-CDs concentration. When HB-CDs concentration reaches 20 mg mL−1, the fluorescence quenching efficiency reaches maximum. As HB-CDs concentration continued to grow, the fluorescence quenching efficiency decreased. Therefore, 20 mg mL−1 was chosen as the optimal HB-CDs concentration for the subsequent experiments. The effect of incubation time on the fluorescence quenching efficiency was investigated by mixing HB-CDs (20 mg mL−1) and Cur (5 μM). The experimental results (Fig. 3b) show that the incubation time has little effect on the experiment in the range of 1–3.5 min. Therefore, 1 min of incubation time is adequate for the analytical procedure.

The sensitivity and the feasibility of the HB-CDs based fluorescence method was further explored. It is evident in Fig. 4 a that with adding of Cur, fluorescence intensity of HB-CDs (425 nm) can be quantitatively quenched; and the fluorescence intensity of Cur (533 nm) was gradually enhanced. There is good linear relationship between the fluorescence quenching degree (425 nm, lg(I0/I)) and Cur concentration within the concentration range of 0.2–200 µM (Fig. 4b). The linear equation is lg(I0/I) = 0.0131C + 0.0053 (µM), and the calculated detection limit (3σ/k) is 20 nM. Here, σ is the standard deviation of the blank solution signals; k is the slope of the calibration curve. The investigation of precision of this fluorescence method (1.02 % of inter-day precision,1.86 % of intra-day precision) showed that the method had good precision.

A comparison between the analytical performance of the reported Cur sensing methods based on CDs probes and this method was made (Details in Table 1 ). It can be concluded that this method possesses a comparable detection limit and a wider linear range. At the same time, for the most popular actual samples (turmeric related food samples), the best Cur extractant from these samples is ethanol [15,17,27,28]. Unfortunately, due to the poor solubility of water-soluble CDs in alcohol solvents, the extraction solvent and fluorescence testing medium in these methods cannot achieve perfect consistency. This inconsistency may lead to complex sample preparation procedures and detection deviations caused by solvent effects. In this work, the alcohol-soluble properties of HB-CDs achieve the solvent consistency of the analyte extraction and fluorescence testing, which dramatically reduces the risk of introducing interfering substances into the detection system due to the difference in solubility, making the selectivity significantly improved. In summary, the combination of the good organic solvent solubility of HB-CDs and the high extraction efficiency of organic solvent for analyte (Cur) not only excludes most possible interfering substances, and greatly improves the analytical selectivity, but also avoids the possible solvent effect for detection in aqueous medium.

According to the sample preparation procedure, Cur was extracted from food with ethanol. Due to the nature of dissolution, metal ions, most carbohydrates, amino acids and other possible interferents cannot enter the ethanol extraction phase. These make the hydrophobic fluorescent probe perfectly cooperate with organic solvent extraction, which not only greatly simplifies the extraction process of the analyte, but also significantly improves the analytical selectivity. To evaluate the selectivity of the HB-CDs probe for Cur detection, the effect of ethanol-soluble potential interfering substances on the determination of Cur was investigated. It can be seen from Fig. 5 that FRU, CA, MA, CPL, and ROX (0.1 mM) had no significant effect on Cur detection. Therefore, the prepared HB-CDs probe possesses high selectivity for Cur in an ethanol medium.

To elucidate the possible quenching mechanism of HB-CDs by Cur, the spectra overlap and fluorescence lifetime for the sensing system were investigated. Obviously, characteristic absorption peak (425 nm) of Cur shows precise overlap with the emission spectrum of HB-CDs (Fig. 6 a). Therefore, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of HB-CDs by Cur may be induced by Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE). The experimental results of fluorescence lifetime measurement showed that the fluorescence lifetime of HB-CDs decreased obviously with the addition of Cur at low (Fig. 6b) and high concentrations (Fig. 6c), which indicated that the interaction mechanism between Cur and HB-CDs may be FRET. At low and high concentrations of Cur, the experimentally observed fluorescence quenching efficiency and the calculated fluorescence quenching efficiency contributed by FRET [44] are listed in Table S1 (including calculations details). Even at high Cur concentration (50 μM), fluorescence quenching efficiency is only 13.0 % contributed from FRET, which indicated that the contribution of FRET in the fluorescence quenching process is small. To further confirm the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the Stern-Volmer plots were investigated, and the obtained curve exhibited an interesting upward curvature, concave towards the y-axis (Fig. 6d). When a fluorescence quenching process involves both dynamic quenching and static quenching, such a characteristic feature of Stern-Volmer plots will be presented [45]. In essence, the fluorescence quenching of HB-CDs by Cur is linked to a combined effect of dynamic quenching and static quenching.

In the design of this project, H2O was introduced into the tested HB-CDs ethanol solution, so that HB-CDs were separated from the solution and reused. As shown in Scheme 1 , when H2O was added to the HB-CDs ethanol solution, the solution immediately became turbid. After centrifugation, it was obvious that the precipitated brown HB-CDs were deposited at the bottom of the test tube. The analytical performance of the recovered HB-CDs was studied. The results showed that when repeated recycling was more than three times, the fluorescence performance and Cur response performance of HB-CDs began to decrease slightly (Fig. S2). The above results show that HB-CDs can be quickly recovered and reused, which can dramatically improve the analysis efficiency and reduce the analysis cost.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cur in food samples
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the practicability of the developed method, the optimized fluorescent sensor was applied to detect the level of Cur in various food samples. Cur was extracted from different food samples by an ethanol extraction procedure, and the determined analytical results (Table 2 ) indicate that mg٠g−1 level of Cur in these food samples were found, and the spiking recoveries range from 83.3 % to 117.8 %. To further verify the accuracy of the method, the above samples were determined by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy. The results (Table S2) showed that the results obtained by UV–vis method were in good agreement with those obtained by fluorescence method. Considering the selectivity and recyclability of HB-CDs probe, the above results show that the developed method possesses high reproducibility, accuracy and cost efficiency.

HB-CDs with high photoluminescence properties were prepared by a solvent-free hydrothermal method using flaxseed oil as a single carbon source. The prepared HB-CDs have a highly selective response to Cur by a combination mechanism of dynamic and static quenching mechanisms. Using ethanol as the analytical medium, the solvent consistency of Cur extraction solvent from foods and detection medium can be achieved, which leads to a high selectivity for the sensing procedure. The developed fluorescence method has been successfully applied to the highly selective determination of Cur in food samples. Coupled with the recyclable and reusable properties of HB-CDs, it has excellent potential for rapid and low-cost quantitative monitoring of Cur in food samples.

Mengyao He: Formal analysis, Methodology, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Xiuming Jiang: Resources. Xinyu Wang: Formal analysis, Visualization. Guoqiang Xiang: . Lijun He: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Innovative Funds Plan of Henan University of Technology (Grant No. 2020ZKCJ03).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.109898.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Curcumin: from ancient medicine to current clinical trials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Hatcher H, Planalp R, Cho J, Torti FM, Torti SV. Curcumin: From ancient medicine to current clinical trials. Cell Mol Life Sci 2008; 65:1631–52. 10.1007/s00018-008-7452-4.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The chemistry of curcumin: from extraction to therapeutic agent

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Priyadarsini K. The Chemistry of Curcumin: From Extraction to Therapeutic Agent. Molecules 2014; 19:20091–112. 10.3390/molecules191220091.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Curcumin in dimethyl sulfoxide: stability, spectral, luminescent and acid-base properties

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Tsaplev YB, Lapina VA, Trofimov AV. Curcumin in dimethyl sulfoxide: Stability, spectral, luminescent and acid-base properties. Dyes and Pigments 2020; 177:108327. 10.1016/j.dyepig.2020.108327.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Improved HPLC method for the determination of curcumin, demethoxycurcumin, and bisdemethoxycurcumin

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Jayaprakasha GK, Jagan Mohan Rao L, Sakariah KK. Improved HPLC Method for the Determination of Curcumin, Demethoxycurcumin, and Bisdemethoxycurcumin. J. Agric. Food Chem. 2002; 50:3668–72. 10.1021/jf025506a.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemical studies on antioxidant mechanism of curcumin: analysis of oxidative coupling products from curcumin and linoleate

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Masuda T, Maekawa T, Hidaka K, Bando H, Takeda Y, Yamaguchi H. Chemical Studies on Antioxidant Mechanism of Curcumin: Analysis of Oxidative Coupling Products from Curcumin and Linoleate. J. Agric. Food Chem. 2001; 49:2539–47. 10.1021/jf001442x.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel bioactivities of curcuma longa constituents

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Roth GN, Chandra A, Nair MG. Novel Bioactivities of Curcuma longa Constituents. J. Nat. Prod. 1998; 61:542–5. 10.1021/np970459f.
Reddy RC, Vatsala PG, Keshamouni VG, Padmanaban G, Rangarajan PN. Curcumin for malaria therapy. Biochem. Biophys. Res. Commun. 2005; 326:472–4. 10.1016/j.bbrc.2004.11.051.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Turmeric and curcumin: biological actions and medicinal applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chattopadhyay I, Biswas K, Bandyopadhyay U, K. Banerjee R. Turmeric and curcumin: Biological Actions and Medicinal Applications. Curr. Sci. 2004; 87:44–53. 10.2307/24107978.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: N-Doped carbon dots for the fluorescence and colorimetry dual-mode detection of curcumin

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yan Y, Zhang H, Du F, Meng Y, Shaomin Shuang, Wang R, et al. N-Doped carbon dots for the fluorescence and colorimetry dual-mode detection of curcumin. Analyst 2021; 146:5357–61. 10.1039/d1an01212f.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of dual-emitting (Gd, Eu)2O3-PEI@CD composite and its potential as ratiometric fluorescent sensor for curcumin

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Guo Z, Zhu Z, Sun Z, Zhang X, Chen Y. Synthesis of dual-emitting (Gd,Eu)2O3-PEI@CD composite and its potential as ratiometric fluorescent sensor for curcumin. Mater Res Bull 2018; 108:83–8. 10.1016/j.materresbull.2018.08.038.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Vortex assisted deep eutectic solvent (DES)-emulsification liquid-liquid microextraction of trace curcumin in food and herbal tea samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Aydin F, Yilmaz E, Soylak M. Vortex assisted deep eutectic solvent (DES)-emulsification liquid-liquid microextraction of trace curcumin in food and herbal tea samples. Food Chem 2018; 243:442–7. 10.1016/j.foodchem.2017.09.154.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Study on the supramolecular interaction of thiabendazole and β-cyclodextrin by spectrophotometry and its analytical application

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Tang B, Wang X, Liang H, Jia B, Chen Z. Study on the Supramolecular Interaction of Thiabendazole and β-Cyclodextrin by Spectrophotometry and Its Analytical Application. J. Agric. Food Chem. 2005; 53:8452–9. 10.1021/jf051683a.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-step synthesis of N-doped carbon dots, and their applications in curcumin sensing, fluorescent inks, and super-resolution nanoscopy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Bu L, Luo T, Peng H, Li L, Long D, Peng J, et al. One-step synthesis of N-doped carbon dots, and their applications in curcumin sensing, fluorescent inks, and super-resolution nanoscopy. Microchim Acta 2019; 186:675. 10.1007/s00604-019-3762-5.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The sensitive fluorimetric method for the determination of curcumin using the enhancement of mixed micelle

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang F, Wu X, Wang F, Liu S, Zeng J, Yang J. The Sensitive Fluorimetric Method for the Determination of Curcumin Using the Enhancement of Mixed Micelle. J Fluoresc 2006; 16:53–9. 10.1007/s10895-005-0025-0.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Rahimi M, Hashemi P, Nazari F. Cold column trapping-cloud point extraction coupled to high performance liquid chromatography for preconcentration and determination of curcumin in human urine. Anal Chim Acta 2014; 826:35–42. 10.1016/j.aca.2014.04.012.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A high-throughput quantification method of curcuminoids and curcumin metabolites in human plasma via high-performance liquid chromatography/tandem mass spectrometry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Cao Y, Xu RX, Liu Z. A high-throughput quantification method of curcuminoids and curcumin metabolites in human plasma via high-performance liquid chromatography/tandem mass spectrometry. J Chromatogr B 2014; 949-950:70–8. 10.1016/j.jchromb.2013.12.039.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multiwalled carbon nanotube modified electrodes for the adsorptive stripping voltammetric determination and quantification of curcumin in turmeric

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chaisiwamongkhol K, Ngamchuea K, Batchelor-McAuley C, Compton RG. Multiwalled Carbon Nanotube Modified Electrodes for the Adsorptive Stripping Voltammetric Determination and Quantification of Curcumin in Turmeric. Electroanal 2016; 29:1049–55. 10.1002/elan.201600670.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel sensitive electrochemical sensor based on nickel chloride solution modified glassy carbon electrode for curcumin determination

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zokhtareh R, Rahimnejad M. A Novel Sensitive Electrochemical Sensor Based on Nickel Chloride Solution Modified Glassy Carbon Electrode for Curcumin Determination. Electroanal 2018; 30:921–7. 10.1002/elan.201700770.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon-dot-based dual-emission silica nanoparticles as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for Bisphenol A

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Gram-scale synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots from locusts for selective determination of sunset yellow in food samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Su K, Xiang G, Jin X, Wang X, Jiang X, He L, et al. Gram‐scale synthesis of nitrogen‐doped carbon dots from locusts for selective determination of sunset yellow in food samples. Luminescence 2021;37:118–26. 10.1002/bio.4152.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-pot hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots and their application for sensitive detection of curcumin and temperature

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Han Z, Zhang H, He L, Pan S, Liu H, Hu X. One-pot hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots and their application for sensitive detection of curcumin and temperature. Microchem J 2019; 146:300–8. 10.1016/j.microc.2019.01.024.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen-doped fluorescence carbon dots as multi-mechanism detection for iodide and curcumin in biological and food samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Tang S, Wang Y, Guo G, Li T, Xing H, Hu H, et al. Activated cascade effect for dual-mode ratiometric and smartphone-assisted visual detection of curcumin and F− based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots. Sci Total Environ 2023; 872:162277. 10.1016/j.scitotenv.2023.162277.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Mirza, Chen Y-C. Bright carbon dots as fluorescence sensing agents for bacteria and curcumin. J Colloid Interf Sci 2017; 501:341–9. 10.1016/j.jcis.2017.04.045.
